title: Land use per kilogram of food product
subtitle: Land use is measured in meters squared (m²) per year to produce one kilogram of a given food product.
caption:
Land use per kilogram of food product

Land use is measured in meters squared (m²) per year to produce one kilogram of a given food product.

This horizontal bar chart ranks twenty-four food products by the land area required to produce one kilogram of each item, measured in square meters per year. The data is sorted in descending order, revealing a wide disparity in land efficiency across different food categories. At the top of the chart, Lamb & Mutton requires the most land at 369.81 m², followed by Beef (beef herd) at 326.21 m². These two categories stand apart from the rest, with bars extending significantly further to the right than any other entry. Cheese follows with 87.79 m², and Dark Chocolate requires 68.96 m². Moving down the list, Beef (dairy herd) sits at 43.24 m², while Coffee requires 21.62 m². A cluster of mid-range values includes Pig Meat (17.36 m²), Nuts (12.96 m²), Poultry Meat (12.22 m²), Groundnuts (9.11 m²), Milk (8.95 m²), and farmed Fish (8.41 m²). The lower section of the chart shows a compression of values where many products require less than 10 m². Peas (7.46 m²), Eggs (6.27 m²), and Wheat & Rye (3.85 m²) occupy the upper end of this lower group. Farmed Prawns (2.97 m²), Maize (2.94 m²), and Rice (2.8 m²) sit just below 3 m². The most land-efficient products appear at the very bottom: Bananas (1.93 m²), Potatoes (0.88 m²), Citrus Fruit (0.86 m²), Tomatoes (0.8 m²), and Apples, which require the least land at 0.63 m². The visual gap between the top two beef and lamb categories and the plant-based products at the bottom illustrates the varying scales of land use intensity.
